prompt: What colour is a typical Ferrari?

options: A supercharged flat-twelve derived from an F1 engine

prompt: The 1967 Le Mans victory by a Ford Mk IV was historic for a very specific reason. What was it?
options: Only all-American victory in Le Mans history — American drivers, team, chassis, engine, and tyres
First car to complete more than 400 laps
The winning car lapped every other competitor at least once

prompt: In rally racing, what does the co-driver's pace note number '6' typically mean?
options: A crest with a hidden landing
A hairpin — the tightest possible turn
A very fast, nearly flat-out bend

prompt: The Citroën DS, launched in 1955, shocked the world with a braking system unlike anything else. What was it?
options: A single central brake pedal controlling individual electric motors
Carbon-ceramic discs on all four wheels
A hydropneumatic system with inboard front disc brakes that also auto-levelled the car
Drum brakes cooled by water jets from an onboard tank

prompt: What does the 'EV' in 'EV charging station' stand for?

options: By bump-starting it down the pit lane
A push-button on the steering wheel after the team primes it in the garage

prompt: Which everyday safety device, now standard worldwide, did Volvo famously patent in 1959 and then let rivals use for free?

options: A historical rule banning right turns
Right turns are illegal under racing flags
The tracks are oval, so all four corners curve the same way

prompt: The 1990s McLaren F1 supercar lined part of its engine bay with real gold. Why?
options: Gold is the best reflector of engine heat, protecting nearby parts
To make it the most expensive car ever sold
Gold conducts the spark to the engine faster
It was purely a status symbol for buyers

prompt: Who coined the term 'horsepower', and why?
options: Gottlieb Daimler, to name his first racing engine
Karl Benz, to measure the pull of his first Motorwagen
James Watt, to sell his steam engines by comparing them to draft horses
Henry Ford, to rate the Model T against a team of oxen

prompt: The classic original Mini, designed by Alec Issigonis, used a clever trick to save space. What was it?
options: The fuel tank doubled as a rear seat
The engine was mounted transversely (sideways) to free up cabin room
It had no doors, just a sliding canopy
The wheels folded inward when parked

prompt: Cruise control was invented in the 1940s by an engineer with a surprising trait. What was it?
options: He had never learned to drive
He had never owned a car

prompt: The Mazda RX-7 and RX-8 are famous for an unusual engine design. What kind?
Why is the Indianapolis 500 winner traditionally handed a bottle of milk in victory lane?
options: Indiana is a major dairy state and lobbied for it
1930s winner Louis Meyer drank buttermilk to cool off on a hot day, and the photo started a tradition
Milk was the only cold drink allowed during Prohibition
It honours a driver who died and whose family ran a dairy

prompt: The Volkswagen logo's 'VW' is well known, but what do the rings of the Audi logo represent?
options: The four wheels of a car
Four founding partners of the company
Four merged car companies that formed Auto Union

prompt: In 1899, the first car ever to break 100 km/h set the record with what kind of power?

options: It ran entirely on solar power
It crossed a continent without refuelling

It reached the speed of a commercial jet at cruise

prompt: The Toyota Corolla holds a notable global distinction among cars. What is it?
options: The only car ever driven to the South Pole
The first car with a built-in airbag
The best-selling car nameplate in the world
